rationale: The citing paper uses Park, Jun, and MacInnis (2000) as core prior evidence that reject-versus-add option framing changes option retention and that rejection decisions are more difficult. Those findings directly motivate the discrepancy manipulation in Study 1 and are revisited when interpreting the initial-set results as consistent with prior option-framing effects. The citation is therefore functionally important to framing hypotheses and experimental setup, not a passing background mention.
relation_description: Wilcox and Song build on Park et al.'s option-framing results in customization (reject vs add) and extend that line by showing how differences in experienced decision difficulty create discrepant-fluency effects across decisions. They add an expectations-based mechanism that predicts downstream changes in feature choice and spending.
